title: The Righteous Brothers

jokes.text: The narrator opening: 'Michael Bluth was working at home when he noticed something unusual.'
jokes.setup: Standard narrator intro in the style of a mundane documentary
jokes.punchline: The 'unusual' thing turns out to be the model home literally sinking — the understatement is the joke
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan understatement is a reliable AD tool. Functional but not exceptional — the house sinking is a decent premise reveal.

jokes.text: The drain pipes aren't hooked up — just empty under the house. Plus there's a lot of blue paint down there. And some pieces of denim?
jokes.setup: Inspector is explaining structural problems with the model home
jokes.punchline: The blue paint and denim are remnants of Tobias's Blue Man Group obsession — a callback to his painting himself blue and sneaking around
jokes.explanation: Exceptional AD craft: a structural inspection of a house inadvertently catalogues the physical evidence of Tobias's secret obsession. The punchline is entirely visual and requires no explanation for fans — the denim is his cutoffs. Michael's 'We've got a guy like that here' is perfectly deadpan. Scores 8.5 because it rewards deep knowledge without alienating newcomers, and the joke is layered across dialogue, visual inference, and character history.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Where the hell do you want me to go? Back to your mother's?' / 'I believe there's a freeloading loser in my bed.' Michael: 'Buster just lost a hand. He's going through a lot.' Lucille: 'I'm talking about Oscar.'
jokes.setup: Michael assumes Lucille is talking about Buster, who lost his hand, as the 'freeloading loser'
jokes.punchline: She's actually talking about Oscar — the misdirect makes us realize Michael (and audience) assumed Buster, revealing how low Buster's standing is in the family
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection built entirely on character logic. Michael's assumption reveals Buster's pathetic status without a single extra word. Strong economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Oscar secretly believes Buster to be his natural son — and greets him warmly saying 'Hey, Uncle.' Oscar replies 'I feel so responsible for that.'
jokes.setup: Narrator reveals Oscar believes he's Buster's real father
jokes.punchline: Buster calls him 'Uncle,' and Oscar says 'I feel so responsible for that' — which reads as guilt about the lost hand but is actually about fathering him
jokes.explanation: A perfectly layered double meaning: Oscar's guilt about the hand injury masks his deeper guilt about being Buster's secret father. The punchline works on first viewing as a hand joke and on rewatch as paternity guilt. Classic AD construction.

jokes.text: Oscar getting injured on a shoddy device George Sr. invented — when he forgot Buster was wearing a prosthetic hand. 'Buster! The Cornballer!'
jokes.setup: The Cornballer is a running gag — George Sr.'s dangerous invention that has burned multiple family members
jokes.punchline: Oscar gets burned because George forgot about the prosthetic hand — the prosthetic saved Buster but displaced the danger to Oscar
jokes.explanation: Smart callback — the prosthetic hand, introduced as tragedy, becomes the pivot for comedy when it accidentally protects Buster and harms Oscar. Adds a new layer to both running gags.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'The only reason she likes him is he has hair. And the only reason he has any is because he's never had any stress.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is bitter that Lucille prefers Oscar
jokes.punchline: He attributes Oscar's full head of hair to a stress-free life — implying George's own baldness is the family's fault, and that Oscar's ease is evidence of his moral failure
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy — George Sr.'s wounded pride and twisted logic are very on-brand. The joke has a nice payoff in the episode's final scene when George shaves Oscar's head, making this retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: It's my father's fault! / My fault? I was trying to keep you from burning yourself, you idiot! / I mean, yes, yes. George Sr. Sure rushed this to market.
jokes.setup: George Sr. defensively protests he was trying to help — a sincere moment.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately confirms 'George Sr. did rush this to market' — validating Oscar's original accusation and undercutting George's defense simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's dry fact-checking of character claims is a signature AD technique. Works well here because George's defensiveness is immediately punctured. Good structure, slightly weaker in originality as it's a repeated device.

jokes.text: George Sr. in prison enthusiastically greeting inmates ('Hey, man! / Yeah!') suggesting he is completely at home there
jokes.setup: George Sr. claims he 'cannot go back to the joint' and that it's a 'hell-hole'
jokes.punchline: Cut to him being warmly greeted by inmates like a beloved regular, totally contradicting his protest
jokes.explanation: A perfect visual juxtaposition joke. The setup (George's protest) is immediately undercut by the visual evidence. Economy is outstanding — no dialogue needed, just the warm greeting. Very much an AD specialty.

jokes.text: George Sr. references 'Dangerous Cousins,' a French movie they tried to sneak into — Michael stammers and denies remembering it.
jokes.setup: George Sr. casually mentions a French film called 'Dangerous Cousins' that he and Michael tried to sneak into as kids
jokes.punchline: Michael awkwardly denies remembering it — but the narrator reveals George Michael has it hidden in his sock drawer, connecting cousin-romance anxiety across generations
jokes.explanation: Exceptional generational layering: the film title 'Dangerous Cousins' mirrors the show's central taboo plotline, Michael's nervous denial implicates him, and George Michael's DVD in his sock drawer reveals the Bluth men share forbidden desires across generations. Three jokes in one beat. Classic AD construction at its best.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It wasn't great, and Maeby knew it.' — Maeby works at the studio that made the American remake of 'Dangerous Cousins'
jokes.setup: The movie premiere is being hyped
jokes.punchline: The narrator matter-of-factly reveals the movie is bad — and Maeby, who works there, already knows this
jokes.explanation: Functional narrator deadpan. Sets up the movie subplot efficiently. Not exceptional but clean.

jokes.text: The studio tried to avoid controversy 'by artlessly explaining that the two leads weren't biological cousins'
jokes.setup: A film about romantic cousins causing controversy
jokes.punchline: The studio's solution is to add a clumsy in-film explanation that the characters aren't really cousins — mirroring AD's own winking at George Michael/Maeby
jokes.explanation: Meta-textual joke — the film-within-the-show mirrors AD's own handling of its cousin romance subplot. The word 'artlessly' is doing a lot of work. Rewarding for attentive viewers.

jokes.text: [Woman On Film] 'Shut up and kiss me!' — played while the studio is panicking about the film's content
jokes.setup: The studio is worried the film is too controversial about cousins
jokes.punchline: The clip playing in the background is the most provocative possible line
jokes.explanation: Functional background gag. Timing is decent but the joke is obvious.

jokes.text: 'But it's only 71 minutes long.' / 'And it's only, like, 63 minutes long.'
jokes.setup: They're told to cut the already short film down
jokes.punchline: The film keeps getting shorter in the same conversation — already 71 minutes, then immediately revised to 63 — suggesting the movie is a disaster being actively dismantled
jokes.explanation: Quick escalation joke — the correction mid-sentence implies the film is literally being cut during the conversation. Nice economy.

jokes.text: 'So bring Ann.' / 'No, that's not her kind of thing.' / 'I mean, if it maintains any of the complex eroticism of the French original anyway.'
jokes.setup: Maeby suggests bringing Ann to a provocative film about cousins
jokes.punchline: George Michael's rejection of Ann is immediately complicated by his acknowledgment of the film's 'complex eroticism' — revealing his own interest
jokes.explanation: George Michael's accidental self-revelation — he dismisses Ann from a sexy film but reveals he knows exactly what makes the original sexy. His awareness of the 'complex eroticism' betrays the DVD in his sock drawer.

jokes.text: 'I like the way they think.' — Maeby, responding to George Michael's 'complex eroticism' comment
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to sound sophisticated about the French film
jokes.punchline: Maeby approves — which is itself slightly disturbing given the cousin dynamic
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient, and a little unsettling. Maeby's casual approval adds another layer to the running cousin-romance theme.

jokes.text: Michael finds Tobias's resignation letter and says 'I'm sorry, Tobias. Do you work here?'
jokes.setup: Tobias has written a dramatic resignation letter
jokes.punchline: Michael doesn't even know Tobias works there — undermining all of Tobias's self-importance in one line
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect line. Maximum economy, devastating to Tobias's sense of self, and entirely in character for both Michael (oblivious to peripheral family chaos) and Tobias (who has somehow been employed without anyone noticing). One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Do I work- I developed an eating disorder being your assistant, Michael.' / Michael: 'I never saw you at the desk.' / Tobias: 'Well, excuse me if I was too busy on my knees in front of the toilet, Michael.'
jokes.setup: Tobias defends his work record by citing his eating disorder
jokes.punchline: His explanation for never being at the desk — being on his knees in front of the toilet — is meant to be sympathetic but sounds like something else entirely in the context of Tobias's many accidental double entendres
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double entendre — the line is completely innocent (eating disorder, bathroom sickness) but lands as something far more suggestive. The layering is perfect: Michael's deadpan 'I never saw you at the desk' sets it up, and Tobias's defence digs deeper.

jokes.text: The Blue Man Group has dropped their cease-and-desist and invited Tobias to audition
jokes.setup: The Blue Man Group had previously sent Tobias a cease-and-desist for painting himself blue
jokes.punchline: Now they've invited him — the absurd reversal rewards viewers who followed the running gag
jokes.explanation: Satisfying payoff to a long-running gag. The reversal (cease-and-desist → invitation) is logical within the show's absurd world.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Tobias was 'asked to address a group of depressed men who had been described over the phone as blue' — cut to Tobias in full blue body paint addressing a depression support group
jokes.setup: Tobias is desperate to join the Blue Man Group; he gets a call about 'blue' men
jokes.punchline: He shows up painted blue to a depression support group because he misunderstood 'blue' (sad) for Blue Man Group blue
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The wordplay ('blue' men = sad men vs. Blue Man Group) is deployed with perfect Tobias-logic — of course he'd show up painted to a depression group. The visual gag of him in full blue paint at a grief circle is hilarious without a word. Character-specific, structurally sound, earned through seasons of setup. Scores high across all metrics.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I feel like a [bleep] idiot.' / Narrator: 'Fortunately, I found a wonderful circle of men to get me through that.'
jokes.setup: Tobias realizes he made a fool of himself at the depression group
jokes.punchline: But those same depressed men became his support group — the humiliation had an upside
jokes.explanation: Functional coda to the preceding joke. The bleeping adds a little texture but the main laugh is already spent.

jokes.text: Tobias booked a 4pm meeting with Kitty without telling Michael. 'Why didn't you tell me?' / 'Oh, this is my fault too?'
jokes.setup: Tobias is supposed to be Michael's assistant and has booked an important meeting
jokes.punchline: He's immediately defensive and makes Michael's legitimate frustration about his own feelings
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias solipsism — he makes every situation about his victimhood. Competent character joke.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Everything- No. No! I will not let this make me eat!' [Sobbing]
jokes.setup: The eating disorder subplot — Tobias developed an eating disorder working for Michael
jokes.punchline: He melodramatically vows not to let the conflict drive him to eat, then immediately collapses into sobs
jokes.explanation: Tobias at his best — the dramatic resolve followed by immediate collapse. The eating disorder gag is unexpected and his self-pity is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: [Narrator] And meetings with Kitty had never ended well. / Say good-bye to these. Spring break! Whoo! / Perhaps this'll jog your memory. / [Cal] Oh! They're crooked!
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up that meetings with Kitty go badly, then cuts to montage-style flashbacks.
jokes.punchline: Kitty's go-to move of flashing is recalled, culminating in Cal's observation 'They're crooked!' — a surprisingly specific and cruel new detail on a known gag.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Kitty's flashing is well-timed, and Cal's critique ('they're crooked!') adds a fresh cruelty to a familiar bit. The escalation from 'this always happens' to a new humiliation of the gag is effective.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Shall I knock Dad out and chain him to a pipe somewhere? Or should I risk another herpes outbreak with Kitty? Which is it this time?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob to help with two problems: Dad and Kitty
jokes.punchline: Gob presents these as equivalent absurd options — one is assault and imprisonment, the other implies a recurring STI from a previous encounter
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Gob — treating violent criminal acts and STIs as interchangeable mild inconveniences. The casualness of 'which is it this time' is perfect. High character integration.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You asked me for $5,000 so that you could invest in a Franklin CD. Now, I assumed that you were talking about the mutual fund and not a compact disc of you singing to your hand.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains why he didn't listen to Gob's CD gift
jokes.punchline: 'A compact disc of you singing to your hand' — the description of Gob's ventriloquism act as 'singing to your hand' is devastatingly accurate and belittling
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed. 'Compact disc of you singing to your hand' reduces Gob's artistic ambitions to their most embarrassing essence. Michael's matter-of-fact delivery makes it land harder. The Franklin/mutual fund setup is efficient misdirection.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Gob made a recording called 'Franklin Comes Alive' — a racial harmony ventriloquism album — and plays a clip: 'It ain't easy bein' white / It ain't easy bein' brown / All this pressure to be bright / I got childrens all over town'
jokes.setup: Gob's ventriloquist dummy Franklin is introduced as a vehicle for racial harmony
jokes.punchline: The song immediately undermines its own thesis by referencing having 'childrens all over town' — irresponsible fatherhood dressed up as racial healing
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The song title 'Franklin Comes Alive' parodies 'Frampton Comes Alive.' The lyrics begin with genuine racial-equality sentiment ('It ain't easy bein' white / It ain't easy bein' brown') then immediately pivot to 'I got childrens all over town' — a comedic non sequitur that exposes Gob's complete lack of self-awareness about what 'racial harmony' means. 'Childrens' is a perfect touch. The ambition (heal the country) vs. execution gap is enormous and hilarious.

jokes.text: It ain't easy bein' white / It ain't easy bein' brown / All this pressure to be bright / I got childrens all over town
jokes.setup: Gob plays a recording of his ventriloquism act to show Michael the CD he made.
jokes.punchline: The song performed by 'Franklin' (Gob's Black puppet) is spectacularly ill-conceived — 'I got childrens all over town' is a racially coded lyric sung by a white man through a Black puppet, the layers of wrongness constituting the comedy.
jokes.explanation: The Franklin act is one of AD's most audacious running gags — a white man performing racial commentary through a Black puppet, completely unaware of the problem. The song lyrics escalate beautifully: starting with a kind of naive both-sides racial equivalence ('it ain't easy bein' white / it ain't easy bein' brown') and then bottoming out with 'I got childrens all over town.' The absurdist racial satire is pointed without being gratuitous — the joke is entirely on Gob's obliviousness. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: 'Sometimes- Hey, where'd the guy go?' — Gob's ventriloquism failing mid-song
jokes.setup: Gob is singing his racial harmony song through Franklin
jokes.punchline: He loses track of the puppet mid-lyric, breaking the fourth wall of his own performance
jokes.explanation: Quick visual/audio gag — the song stops and Gob immediately can't find his own puppet. Reinforces his incompetence. Good economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Maybe the reason I didn't say anything is 'cause I didn't want to embarrass you. It's my favorite, favorite record.' Gob: 'Well, then you [bleep] up.' [Sobbing]
jokes.setup: Michael tries to spare Gob's feelings by saying the CD is his favorite
jokes.punchline: Gob is devastated that Michael kept his love secret — the attempted kindness makes Gob more upset than the truth would have
jokes.explanation: Wonderful reversal — Michael's white lie backfires in the most Gob-specific way. The absurd logic (if you loved it, you should have said so) paired with immediate sobbing is strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Crying like a couple of girls.' Michael: 'Aw, you're the only one crying. But I love the music.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael are having a bonding moment over the CD
jokes.punchline: Gob thinks they're both crying; Michael gently corrects him — only Gob is crying — while maintaining that he loves the music
jokes.explanation: Michael's gentle correction is perfectly understated — he lets Gob have the emotional moment while quietly noting the asymmetry. Good economy and character.

jokes.text: Michael: 'And the card with your face on it?' Gob: 'And the healing.' Michael: 'Home run!'
jokes.setup: Michael is listing what he loves about the CD gift
jokes.punchline: 'Home run!' is Michael's totally ironic, slightly forced enthusiasm — he's humoring Gob in the most transparent way
jokes.explanation: Michael's mock-enthusiasm is charming. 'Home run!' is a funny non-sequitur energy level that suggests he's running out of things to praise.

jokes.text: George Sr. meets Franklin the puppet: 'Hey, Franklin.' Franklin: 'My man! How 'bout some tongue?'
jokes.setup: Gob introduces Franklin to his father as part of their working arrangement
jokes.punchline: Franklin immediately asks George Sr. for 'some tongue' — the puppet's aggressive personality clashes wonderfully with the formal family reunion
jokes.explanation: Franklin's personality is perfectly established — forward, inappropriate, and socially unfiltered in ways Gob isn't. The tongue request as a greeting is a great non sequitur.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But Franklin didn't want to kiss at all. Gob had soaked the puppet's mouth in ether.'
jokes.setup: Gob needs to knock out George Sr. without getting caught
jokes.punchline: He has soaked Franklin's mouth in ether — so when Franklin 'kisses' George Sr., he'll be knocked unconscious. The narrator reports this as a simple fact.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. The concept alone — soaking a ventriloquist puppet's mouth in ether so it can administer a kiss-based chloroforming — is uniquely insane. The narrator's completely flat delivery ('But Franklin didn't want to kiss at all') makes it even funnier. Only Gob could conceive this plan. Maximum originality, structure, character integration, and economy. One of the show's great jokes.

jokes.text: Tobias and Lindsay subplot: Lindsay refuses to uproot her life for Vegas; Tobias says maybe this means they should split; Lindsay thought they already were split
jokes.setup: Tobias announces his Vegas opportunity
jokes.punchline: Neither of them knows whether they're together or broken up — the marriage is so dysfunctional they can't track its status
jokes.explanation: Well-observed cringe comedy about a marriage so broken neither party can track its state. The escalating confusion ('Maybe this means we should split' / 'I thought we were split') is nicely handled.

jokes.text: Gob using ventriloquism to make George Sr. 'forgive' him during the drive: [imitating George Sr.] 'I cherish you, Gob. Come here and give me a kiss.' / 'I'm driving, Dad.' / [As George Sr.] 'Now!'
jokes.setup: Gob is driving an unconscious or restrained George Sr. to the police station
jokes.punchline: He uses ventriloquism to give himself the paternal validation he desperately needs — with George Sr.'s voice demanding a kiss
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary character moment. Gob's need for paternal approval is so pathological that he ventriloquizes his father's forgiveness — and his refusal ('I'm driving, Dad') treats the imaginary conversation as completely real. The narrator's deadpan 'It was weird' undersells it perfectly. Deeply specific to Gob's psychology.

jokes.text: Gob discovers Michael's unopened CD in the car: 'It's not even open. Michael!'
jokes.setup: Michael claimed to love the CD; it was supposedly his favorite
jokes.punchline: The CD is still wrapped — Gob finds it in the car immediately after Michael's profession of love
jokes.explanation: Sharp payoff to the CD subplot — and this single moment will later become a key plot point (the card on the CD is used as photographic evidence). Economical and funny.

jokes.text: Kitty's rehabilitation: 'I have a sponsor now. [Whispers] He's famous.' / 'That's great.' / 'I can't tell you who he is. But let's just say that he was on Night Court.'
jokes.setup: Kitty claims to be in recovery and has a famous sponsor
jokes.punchline: The 'famous' sponsor is someone from 'Night Court' — a very specific, slightly obscure NBC sitcom from the '80s, deflating the 'famous' claim
jokes.explanation: The comedy of declining celebrity — 'Night Court' is famous enough to be recognizable but obscure enough to be funny. The whispering builds the joke well.

jokes.text: Kitty helpfully narrows it down: 'It's not Bull. It's not Harry Anderson. He's white.' Michael: 'I know who it is.'
jokes.setup: Kitty is trying not to reveal her sponsor's identity
jokes.punchline: Her attempts to narrow it down (not Bull, not Harry Anderson, he's white) actually tell Michael exactly who it is — the secret is poorly kept
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the Night Court joke — Kitty's clues are so specific that they inadvertently identify the person. The structure of elimination (not Bull, not Harry Anderson, white) is clean and escalating.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'Don't suppose I can get that in writing.' Michael: 'You did try to blow me up.' Kitty: 'Sorry about that. Just don't piss me off again.' [Cackles]
jokes.setup: Kitty says she won't try to hurt the Bluths anymore
jokes.punchline: The immediate qualifier 'just don't piss me off again' and the maniacal cackle undercut any sincere rehabilitation
jokes.explanation: Efficient character joke — Kitty's reform is immediately and hilariously conditional. The cackle sells it.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Oh, I see you wasted no time in filling my seat hole.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just resigned and Kitty is sitting in his chair
jokes.punchline: 'Seat hole' — Tobias's accidental double entendre turns a mundane observation into something that sounds wildly inappropriate
jokes.explanation: A textbook Tobias double entendre. 'Seat hole' is grammatically correct (it means the chair's vacancy) but sounds obscene. Brief, punchy, perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'It's the last time you're going to be seeing these.' [goes to flash] Tobias: 'How ironic. I just found out I'm staying.'
jokes.setup: Kitty's recurring 'say goodbye to these' flash is a running gag
jokes.punchline: This time the flash is directed at Tobias and Michael simultaneously — Tobias's immediate 'How ironic, I just found out I'm staying' is perfectly timed
jokes.explanation: A great variation on the running gag — previously deployed as threat/seduction, now directed at Tobias in a moment of accidental voyeurism. His timing (just found out he's staying) elevates it from straight callback to structured joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Okay, let's just all put our tops down. Just put 'em on down.'
jokes.setup: Kitty is flashing; Michael needs to manage the situation
jokes.punchline: Michael's attempt at diplomacy — treating a flashing incident as a minor social awkwardness to be managed calmly — is perfectly understated
jokes.explanation: Michael's measured 'let's all put our tops down' is characteristically composed and funny — he's dealing with workplace nudity like it's a minor PowerPoint issue.

jokes.text: Tobias to Kitty: 'Wow, you are so real. Why can't I meet someone like you?'
jokes.setup: Kitty has just flashed Tobias; he's responding to her
jokes.punchline: Tobias is so starved for authentic human connection that he finds the most unstable, hostile woman in the room 'real' — and immediately wants to date her
jokes.explanation: Deeply Tobias — he evaluates people by their emotional intensity rather than their sanity. The irony is multilayered: Kitty just threatened everyone in the room.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Are you trying to set up your former assistant with your current assistant? What a great idea for a Hugh Grant-Julia Roberts type movie.'
jokes.setup: Michael engineers a date between Tobias and Kitty
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately pitches it as a Hugh Grant-Julia Roberts film — and then suggests John Larroquette for the lead, drastically downgrading the casting
jokes.explanation: A two-beat joke: first the meta-film reference (Tobias sees everything as a movie pitch), then the immediate downgrade from Hugh Grant to John Larroquette. Both beats work and the second amplifies the first.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I recently heard that the actor, John Larroquette, was looking for a meaty character piece.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just suggested a Hugh Grant-Julia Roberts film
jokes.punchline: He immediately replaces that vision with John Larroquette seeking 'a meaty character piece' — an enormous downgrade delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The callback to Night Court is delicious — Larroquette was on Night Court, tying the joke back to Kitty's sponsor subplot. Tobias's deadpan sincerity about the casting downgrade is very funny.

jokes.text: Police officer: 'Are those balls? 'Cause the last time we were here, they were balls.' / 'These are not balls.' Michael: 'I wish they were balls.'
jokes.setup: Police arrive with what looks like evidence photos
jokes.punchline: The officer references a previous visit where the photos turned out to be Kitty's breasts — Michael wishes this time were the same, as balls would be preferable to incriminating photos
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback execution. 'I wish they were balls' is a perfect line — Michael wishing the evidence were something inappropriate rather than genuinely incriminating. The season-long callback to Kitty's photo stunt is efficiently deployed. 'Balls' is used with admirable precision.

jokes.text: I wish they were balls.
jokes.setup: Barry's initial hope that the photos were the (familiar) inappropriate ones is dashed — they're incriminating traffic camera photos instead.
jokes.punchline: Barry's resigned wish — 'I wish they were balls' — is the perfect capper to the previous joke. He'd rather deal with genital photos than actual evidence of his client's crimes.
jokes.explanation: Five words that contain multitudes. 'I wish they were balls' is a perfect comedy sentence — it has the prior joke as context, it reveals Barry's warped legal priorities, and it works as pure absurdism. The economy score is a 10 because you cannot trim a single word. This is the kind of line that becomes a cult touchstone for fans of the show. The callback structure (setup → false hope → punchline → callback echo) is textbook perfect.

jokes.text: The incriminating photo is actually the card from Gob's unopened CD — the card with Gob's face on it, found by a traffic camera
jokes.setup: Michael is identified in a car with his father via traffic camera photo
jokes.punchline: The photo is actually the card from Gob's CD gift — Gob's face on the card, sitting on the dashboard — not Michael holding a photo of himself
jokes.explanation: One of AD's signature 'everything connects' payoffs. The CD card — set up as a comedy prop about Gob's wounded pride — becomes the legal instrument that destroys Michael. Multiple threads (the CD subplot, the photo evidence, the card with Gob's face) converge perfectly. This is what the show does better than almost anyone.

jokes.text: Gob in jail with Michael: 'Right between the gearbox and the 'pull back from aircraft' lever.'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining where he found the unopened CD
jokes.punchline: The 'pull back from aircraft' lever is a casual reference to a completely absurd feature of Gob's vehicle — the stair car — delivered as if it's perfectly normal
jokes.explanation: The stair car's 'pull back from aircraft' lever is a beautiful detail — Gob operates a vehicle designed for airplane boarding and treats it as mundane transportation. The specificity of the detail is what makes it land.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I've got him stashed away somewhere safe now.' / Michael: 'Get him, quickly, otherwise I'm gonna be stuck in here.' / Gob: 'Good! I hope you rot in here!'
jokes.setup: Michael needs Gob to produce their father to clear his name
jokes.punchline: Gob's cooperation evaporates when he remembers the CD — he immediately turns on Michael
jokes.explanation: Gob's petty pivot from brotherly assistance to abandonment over the CD is perfectly in character. Quick and effective.

jokes.text: Gob: 'And guess what else? Dad kissed me.' Michael: 'How? He looked pretty unconscious in that picture.' Gob: 'I didn't say he was totally into it.'
jokes.setup: Gob reveals George Sr. kissed him in the car
jokes.punchline: 'I didn't say he was totally into it' — the admission that the kiss was delivered to an unconscious man, and Gob's casualness about this, is the joke
jokes.explanation: A top-tier AD line. Michael's straight-man question ('How? He looked pretty unconscious') prompts the reveal: Gob kissed his ether-unconscious father and characterized the lack of enthusiasm as 'not totally into it.' The casual understatement about the ethics of the situation is breathtaking. Extremely quotable and perfectly structured.

jokes.text: Ann: 'No, I want to get the whole church group together. We're gonna picket those bastards.' Narrator: 'George Michael had only heard Ann swear once before...'
jokes.setup: Ann is normally extremely prim and religious
jokes.punchline: She drops 'bastards' with complete conviction — and the narrator reveals this is only her second swear, which was also at a protest
jokes.explanation: Ann's righteous profanity is a great character joke — she swears only in the service of God's wrath. The narrator callback to the previous instance structures it as a pattern.

jokes.text: Narrator: Ann's previous swear was at a protest of Marc Cherry's home, executive producer of Desperate Housewives. Cut to Ann: 'It's a satire!' / George Michael: 'There's nothing funny about fornication!' / Ann: 'The bastard saw us!'
jokes.setup: Flashback to Ann's previous protest — of Marc Cherry's house
jokes.punchline: George Michael has adopted Ann's religious language; 'It's a satire!' is Marc Cherry defending his show; 'The bastard saw us!' is Ann's outraged return swear at being spotted
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly layered: a meta-reference (AD mocking a rival show), character comedy (George Michael saying 'there's nothing funny about fornication'), and Ann's contradictory 'bastard saw us' — she swears while protesting fornication. The George Michael line in particular is exceptional — he's parroting Ann's morality while his own desires are thoroughly compromised.

jokes.text: 'And the promise of another kiss was too enticing to turn down.' George Michael: 'Well, yes. The bastards.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is tempted by the promise of a kiss from Ann at the protest
jokes.punchline: He parrots 'The bastards' with zero conviction — he's only protesting to stay close to Ann
jokes.explanation: George Michael's half-hearted adoption of Ann's rhetoric, motivated purely by romantic opportunism, is a wonderfully honest character beat. Very economical — 'Well, yes. The bastards.' does a lot of work.

jokes.text: Oscar: 'Buster! Be careful!' Buster: 'It's not a real hand, remember?' Oscar: 'I'm so forgetful. I hope you don't get that from me.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is concerned about Buster's prosthetic hand near the Cornballer
jokes.punchline: 'I hope you don't get that from me' — Oscar's casual aside about forgetfulness reveals he's thinking about being Buster's real father
jokes.explanation: Elegant double meaning — on the surface, Oscar is just being self-deprecating. But 'I hope you don't get that from me' is the paternity secret escaping. Works on both levels simultaneously, which is AD at its best.

jokes.text: Oscar burns himself on the Cornballer after Buster's prosthetic redirects the danger: '[Sizzling] [Screams] Just forget it! I wanted to share my Pop Secret with you. Oh, but forget it!'
jokes.setup: Oscar is trying to have a vulnerable conversation with Buster about being his real father ('Pop Secret')
jokes.punchline: 'Pop Secret' is both the popcorn brand and a reference to his secret about being Buster's pop (father) — the double meaning is the joke
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke. 'Pop Secret' works as a brand name (they're making popcorn near the Cornballer), as a pun on 'pop' meaning father, and as a reference to the actual secret Oscar is trying to share. Three meanings, zero wasted words, entirely character-motivated, and the physical comedy of the Cornballer burn is the perfect punctuation. One of the episode's very best moments and among the show's finest wordplay constructions.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Pop secret? Pop se- Is Oscar my real father?'
jokes.setup: Oscar has stormed off after shouting 'Pop Secret'
jokes.punchline: Buster slowly parses the double meaning and arrives at the conclusion — the audience watches him connect the dots in real time
jokes.explanation: The comedic payoff of the 'Pop Secret' joke — Buster's dawning realization, with his characteristic slowness, watching him parse the pun aloud. The stammer ('Pop se-') is perfect timing.

jokes.text: Michael calls Lucille while in police custody to lie about being 'on a business trip' — Lucille: 'Why can't the girl at work do it?' Michael: 'Tobias? I sent him on a date with Kitty to keep her off our backs.'
jokes.setup: Michael calls Lucille from jail as if on a business trip
jokes.punchline: The detail that Michael sent Tobias 'on a date with Kitty' — treating his brother-in-law like an expendable pawn — is casually devastating
jokes.explanation: Functional exposition delivery that contains a funny character beat. Michael's casual deployment of Tobias as a Kitty distraction is very in-character.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Your husband's dating Kitty the whore.' / Lindsay: 'He is? That's- That's horrible.' / Lucille: 'Do I know my daughter? She only wants what she can't have.'
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Lindsay that Tobias is dating Kitty
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's horror is not about betrayal but about competition — and Lucille correctly diagnoses that Lindsay's sudden interest in Tobias is purely because someone else has him
jokes.explanation: Lucille's perceptive cynicism about Lindsay's motivations is sharply observed and entirely true. 'She only wants what she can't have' is a devastating psychological diagnosis delivered as casual small talk.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I've got a nice, hard cot with his name on it.' Gob: 'You'd do that to your own brother?' Lucille: 'I said 'cot.''
jokes.setup: Lucille threatens to implicate Gob so he'll join Michael in jail
jokes.punchline: 'I said 'cot'' — the punchline implies everyone heard something else; Lucille's correction is more damning than innocent
jokes.explanation: Extremely economical, extremely dark. 'I said cot' is one of the most efficient dirty-joke-deniability constructions in the show. Lucille's affect — completely straight-faced — makes it land harder. Gob's horrified 'You'd do that to your own brother?' is the perfect setup for the correction.

jokes.text: Buster arrives: 'Where's Mother?' / Lindsay: 'You lied to me. You said my father was my father, but my uncle is my father. My father is my uncle!'
jokes.setup: Buster has just processed the Oscar revelation
jokes.punchline: His circular, escalating confusion — 'my father is my uncle' — captures the absurdity of the Bluth family tree in one perfectly confused sentence
jokes.explanation: 'My father is my uncle' is a quotable distillation of the entire Bluth family dysfunction. Buster's existential confusion is perfectly rendered. High character integration.

jokes.text: Guards play Gob's confiscated CD — the racial harmony ventriloquism album — in the prison. Michael hears it and realizes he can't turn Gob in.
jokes.setup: Michael has been debating whether to testify against Gob
jokes.punchline: The CD — the object of the entire subplot — turns out to be the emotional catalyst that saves their relationship, played by guards who confiscated it
jokes.explanation: A beautiful convergence — the CD that caused Michael's arrest (via the card) is also the thing that reconciles him with Gob. The guards playing it as entertainment in the police station is the absurdist grace note. The album's stated purpose ('heal this country') is achieved, just not in the way Gob imagined.

jokes.text: Gob to Franklin after the moment of brotherly epiphany: 'Franklin.' Franklin (British accent): 'You've ruined the act, Gob.'
jokes.setup: Gob has had a sincere emotional moment about brotherhood
jokes.punchline: Franklin responds in a British accent — completely incongruous for a Black ventriloquist character — to accuse Gob of ruining the act
jokes.explanation: The British accent on Franklin is a left-field absurdist choice that suggests Gob can't maintain any character consistently. 'You've ruined the act, Gob' is oddly poignant coming from a hand puppet. Extremely economical and unexpected.

jokes.text: Oscar and Lucille argue: 'You're high!' / 'You're drunk!' / 'Not this time.'
jokes.setup: Oscar and Lucille are having an emotional confrontation
jokes.punchline: 'Not this time' — Lucille's clarification implies she is often drunk during their arguments, and she's only sober now because of genuine anger
jokes.explanation: The exchange works on character economy — two lines that expose the entire texture of Oscar and Lucille's relationship. 'Not this time' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Lucille threatens to 'say goodbye to these' at Oscar
jokes.setup: Lucille is ending her confrontation with Oscar dramatically
jokes.punchline: She borrows Kitty's signature move — threatening to flash someone — in a moment of escalating absurdity
jokes.explanation: The running gag passes to Lucille, which is an unexpected character transfer. Slightly less effective than previous deployments but the shift to Lucille adds an unexpected layer.

jokes.text: George Michael is holding a protest sign that says 'This is a tricky, gray area.' Ann: 'He even helped me make the signs.' George Michael: 'Well, only mine and the one that says, 'This is a tricky, gray area.''
jokes.setup: George Michael is nominally protesting the cousin-romance film
jokes.punchline: His protest sign — 'This is a tricky, gray area' — is a perfect encapsulation of his moral conflict about Maeby, turned into a sign he carries while 'protesting' a film about his exact situation
jokes.explanation: This is arguably the best joke in the episode and one of the show's finest. George Michael's sign ('This is a tricky, gray area') at a protest of a film about cousin romance perfectly externalizes his internal conflict about Maeby. He is literally carrying his own confession as a placard. The specificity (he only helped make his sign and this one) reveals he couldn't bring himself to write a sign condemning something he's guilty of. Multiple layers: meta-commentary, character psychology, visual gag, and irony all converge in four words on a piece of cardboard.

jokes.text: 'Are there any more seats?' / 'Yeah. It's the best 52 minutes you'll spend all day.'
jokes.setup: The protest is drawing attention to the film
jokes.punchline: The film keeps getting shorter — now 52 minutes — as the protest inadvertently sells tickets
jokes.explanation: The film's runtime continues its absurd descent (71 → 63 → 52 minutes). The salesman pivot from protester to ticket-seller is sharp. Running gag with escalation.

jokes.text: Ann: 'Oh, no. We're making it worse.' / Ann: 'Prepare to be swallowed into the depths of hell!'
jokes.setup: The protest is drawing lines for the film rather than deterring viewers
jokes.punchline: Ann's response to failure is to escalate to apocalyptic threats — 'Prepare to be swallowed into the depths of hell!'
jokes.explanation: Ann's escalation from strategic protest to hell-threat is perfectly in character — her response to setbacks is to invoke divine punishment. The gap between her gentle demeanor and violent rhetoric is the joke.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I guess that means no kiss?' Ann: 'I'm not really in the mood for a kiss.' George Michael: 'This isn't Marc Cherry's house.'
jokes.setup: Ann is furious at the protest's failure
jokes.punchline: 'This isn't Marc Cherry's house' — George Michael references the previous flashback protest where a kiss was promised, trying to replicate the conditions
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — George Michael connects the locations of protest with the promise of a kiss, revealing that his protest activity is entirely motivated by romantic opportunism. 'This isn't Marc Cherry's house' is a perfectly weird, specific line that only makes sense within the episode's internal logic.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Guess who I just found out my father is?' Buster: 'Jail?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is processing the news that George Sr. is in jail; Buster is processing the Oscar revelation
jokes.punchline: Buster mishears or misunderstands Lindsay's question about paternity — answering 'Jail?' because that's what's on his mind — the crossed wires are perfectly Buster
jokes.explanation: Perfectly constructed crossed-wires joke. Lindsay asks 'who is my father?' and Buster — preoccupied with George Sr.'s arrest — answers 'Jail?' as if jail is a person. Two different conversations perfectly colliding. Extremely quotable and economical.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I'm going to take a long shower. I don't even want to smell like Mother anymore.'
jokes.setup: Buster has just discovered Oscar might be his father — a revelation about his unusual closeness to his mother
jokes.punchline: His response is to scrub off his mother — a deeply Freudian, deeply Buster reaction to finding out the family secret
jokes.explanation: The line is devastatingly character-specific — Buster's codependence with Lucille is so total that his response to an existential revelation is to physically separate from her smell. 'I don't even want to smell like Mother anymore' is one of the episode's best lines, layering Freudian humor with genuine character pathos.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I pushed him right into someone else's skanky, bony arms.' / Advice to George Michael: 'You have to grab love while you have it. Don't let it get away.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is drunk and lamenting that Tobias is with Kitty
jokes.punchline: She immediately dispenses romantic advice to George Michael — advice she herself spectacularly failed to follow — with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony of Lindsay's advice is the joke — she's dispensing wisdom she completely lacks. Works in context but requires character knowledge.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Mommy's gonna take a bath with a bottle of wine.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is in a spiral over Tobias
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'bath with a bottle of wine' as a coping mechanism — announced to her teenage daughter — is both sad and funny
jokes.explanation: A Lindsay line through and through — her chosen response to emotional pain is wine and a bath, and she announces it to her child. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'I don't know if we're supposed to use-' — cut off by Maeby with news about the premiere
jokes.setup: Lindsay announces a bath; someone starts to warn about the pipes
jokes.punchline: The callback to the compromised plumbing is cut off mid-sentence — another brush with the sinking-house problem
jokes.explanation: A very brief background joke about the pipes — more of a structural threading than a full joke. Low impact but tracks the episode's running problem.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'All the press actually made the premiere a hit. There's lines for the next show.'
jokes.setup: The protest was supposed to stop the film; Maeby has been worried about losing her job
jokes.punchline: The protest made the film successful — the Streisand Effect as a plot resolution
jokes.explanation: The irony of the protest backfiring and saving the film is functional but not especially novel. The payoff to the subplot is satisfying without being brilliant.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And that's when George Michael finally got close to Maeby — who, by the way, might not be his biological cousin.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby share a moment at the premiere
jokes.punchline: 'Who, by the way, might not be his biological cousin' — the narrator casually drops this reassurance/complication in a parenthetical, as if it's a minor plot footnote
jokes.explanation: The narrator's casual 'by the way' about whether George Michael's crush is his biological cousin is a masterclass in AD's voice. The 'by the way' implies this is a minor clarification while actually being the central moral question of the subplot. The show winking at itself while pretending not to.

jokes.text: Gob hid George Sr. under the model home — the home that has just collapsed. 'Dad's gonna be crushed.' / 'You don't have to tell him!' / Narrator: 'But Gob was referring to his own father, who he had hidden under the house.'
jokes.setup: Gob says 'Dad's gonna be crushed' — seemingly about George Sr.'s emotional reaction
jokes.punchline: Gob means literally crushed — he hid him under the house that just collapsed. The narrator corrects the misdirection flatly.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding misdirection. 'Dad's gonna be crushed' reads as emotional; the narrator's correction reveals it's literal. The pun is earned by plot logic, not word scrambling. One of the episode's best constructions. The child's 'You don't have to tell him!' responding to the emotional reading while the narrator clarifies the physical reality creates a perfect three-part structure.

jokes.text: Gob asks children to crawl under the collapsed house. 'Children, you're small. Crawl under there.' / 'We don't want to go under there.' / 'He's not under there. He got away. Thank God.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. may be trapped under the collapsed house
jokes.punchline: Gob briefly considers sending children in to retrieve his father, then immediately announces (with relief) that the father escaped — a relief motivated by avoiding criminal liability, not parental concern
jokes.explanation: Gob's willingness to send anonymous children into a collapsed structure before pivoting to relief is very in character — his moral calculations are consistently insane.

jokes.text: Children, you're small. Crawl under there.
jokes.setup: The house has collapsed and Gob needs to find his father underneath it.
jokes.punchline: Gob's solution is to send children into the rubble, which is both practically logical and morally appalling in a way only Gob could execute with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: Gob's pragmatic cruelty to children is perfectly in character — he genuinely doesn't register why this is wrong. 'You're small' as the qualification is the comedic engine. Character integration is near-perfect: this could only be Gob. Highly quotable because the logic is so confident.

jokes.text: Then Gob remembered that this meant Michael would have to go to prison — a moment of dawning horror at his own competence
jokes.setup: Gob is relieved George Sr. escaped
jokes.punchline: Then he realizes that without George Sr., Michael has no alibi and will go to jail — Gob's relief immediately turns to horror at the consequences of his luck
jokes.explanation: A satisfying beat — Gob's relief becomes its own problem. The joke is in the speed of his reversal.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, I handcuffed him under the house. But he got free — the pipes I chained him to weren't connected to anything.'
jokes.setup: Gob was supposed to secure George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. escaped because the pipes under the model home weren't connected — the same structural flaw established in the opening scene is the reason Gob's plan failed
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural payoffs. The disconnected pipes — established as a comic structural problem in the opening scene — become the cause of George Sr.'s escape and Michael's legal jeopardy. This is AD's trademark: every setup has a payoff, every detail matters. Exceptional episode architecture.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm sick of you needing constant validation for screwing up.'
jokes.setup: Gob expects a 'thank you' for trying to help, even though he failed catastrophically
jokes.punchline: Michael's exasperation crystallizes in a single line that's devastating and completely accurate
jokes.explanation: Sharp, quotable, character-specific. 'Constant validation for screwing up' perfectly describes Gob's entire relationship with the family. Michael's frustration compressed into one punchy line.

jokes.text: Gob's threat: 'First, I'm gonna beat the crap out of you, then I'm gonna call an ambulance. Then knowing you, I'm gonna have to wait for some kind of an apology.'
jokes.setup: Gob threatens Michael for not appreciating his efforts
jokes.punchline: The threat escalates past the violence to the anticipated post-fight social obligations — 'knowing you, I'm gonna have to wait for some kind of an apology' — making the apology-waiting the real grievance
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke structure. The threat (beat you up) + consequence (call ambulance) + anticipated grievance (wait for apology) is perfectly Gob. The third beat — the apology-waiting — is the unexpected escalation that makes it exceptional. Gob's logic (violence is fine; lack of acknowledgment is the real problem) is entirely consistent with his character. Very quotable.

jokes.text: [Grunts] — Gob actually attacks Michael in front of the courthouse
jokes.setup: Gob has been threatening Michael
jokes.punchline: He actually follows through — attacking his brother in front of the courthouse with his arraignment pending
jokes.explanation: The physical follow-through is funny in context but less notable than the preceding joke. The humor is primarily in the setup (joke 83).

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I know you're in the middle of something, but I'm afraid I can't work for you today. I'm going to Vegas with Kitty.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are literally brawling in front of the courthouse
jokes.punchline: Tobias interrupts a fistfight to announce he's quitting — treating this as an appropriate moment for a professional conversation
jokes.explanation: Tobias's obliviousness to the physical chaos around him while discussing HR matters is very characteristic. The timing — interrupting an active fistfight with an employment notice — is the joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay arrives: 'Over my dead body!' — rushing to fight for Tobias
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been indifferent to Tobias throughout the episode — now she's furious he's leaving
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic arrival to fight for a man she ignored confirms Lucille's diagnosis: she only wants what she can't have
jokes.explanation: The payoff to Lucille's diagnosis — Lindsay's sudden ferocity for a husband she's been dismissing proves the point. Functionally satisfying.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I have torn this family apart with my lies and my cowardice, but that comes to an end now. So I'm turning myself in. I don't want you to come and visit me in prison this time. 'Cause you deserve Oscar now.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. delivers a sincere speech about family sacrifice
jokes.punchline: 'You deserve Oscar now' — his 'gift' to Lucille is her other lover, which simultaneously makes his sacrifice sound generous and reveals he knows about Oscar's relationship with her
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s magnanimous 'sacrifice' undercut by the specificity of 'you deserve Oscar now' — he knows and is dispensing with the pretense in the most passive-aggressive way possible.

jokes.text: 'Cause you deserve Oscar now.
jokes.setup: George Sr. says he's turning himself in and tells Lucille she deserves Oscar.
jokes.punchline: His 'you deserve Oscar' is framed as a gift/sacrifice but reads as an insult — Oscar is his less successful twin, and George is essentially saying 'you deserve to downgrade to my brother.' The dark selfishness beneath the apparent nobility is pure George Sr.
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s farewell gift of 'you deserve Oscar' is perfectly ambiguous — is it generosity or a parting insult? Given his character, it's both. The economy of a single sentence doing dual emotional work is good writing.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Tobias, I actually fought for you.' / 'I can't believe it.' / [chuckles] / 'I'm going to Vegas with Kitty.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just fought for Tobias, declaring she wants him
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response — a brief pause, apparent touching moment, then 'I'm going to Vegas with Kitty' — is the ultimate Tobias move: he finally gets what he wanted (Lindsay fighting for him) and immediately sabotages it
jokes.explanation: A devastating Tobias character moment. He's wanted Lindsay's love and attention, she finally delivers it, and his response is to reject her. The pause before 'I'm going to Vegas with Kitty' is perfect — a beat of apparent reflection followed by the most self-defeating choice. This is among the episode's best character jokes.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Oh, George, I've lost you again.' Oscar: 'Take back Oscar, Mother.' Lindsay: 'I think I lost him too.'
jokes.setup: The family is reacting to the various relationship collapses
jokes.punchline: Three simultaneous losses — Lucille mourns George, someone tells her to take Oscar back, Lindsay realizes Tobias is gone — the cascade of failed relationships is the joke
jokes.explanation: A decent ensemble moment cataloguing losses. The rapid-fire parallel structure works but doesn't have a sharp punchline.

jokes.text: George Sr. approaches Oscar: 'I want you to turn me in so you can get the reward, take care of the family.' / Oscar: 'Why would you do that for me?' / George: 'Because we're brothers.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. makes an apparently selfless offer
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately reveals George's real plan — to shave Oscar's head and impersonate him in prison, getting away while Oscar serves his time
jokes.explanation: A perfect George Sr. moment — the language of brotherhood and sacrifice deployed as a con. 'Because we're brothers' as setup for identity theft via head-shaving is a glorious reversal of the episode's theme.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Later, while George Sr. was shaving his unconscious brother's head, he wondered whether there really was a reward, and if he could get it.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has framed the head-shaving as a sacrifice for family
jokes.punchline: He's wondering if he can also collect the reward money — greed compounded on top of identity theft
jokes.explanation: The narrator's calm recounting of George shaving an unconscious man's head while calculating reward money is darkly perfect. Adds a layer of greed to the con. The deadpan delivery elevates it.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, I didn't lose him. But he's all puckered and white. On the plus side, you can take him to lunch at the club now.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he's lost Franklin (the puppet) — ether damaged Franklin's mouth
jokes.punchline: 'All puckered and white' — the ether has bleached/damaged the Black puppet's face; Gob's silver lining is that a white puppet can now access places a Black puppet can't
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes and among AD's most daring. The ether bleached Franklin's face, and Gob's consolation is that a whitened Franklin can now 'go to the club' — exposing the racism of the club with deadpan horror. The joke is simultaneously a callback (ether/Franklin's mouth), a dark social commentary (club racism), and a quintessential Gob observation (he sees the upside). Gob is not the satirical voice here — the joke satirizes the club. Extremely quotable and deeply uncomfortable in the best way.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That's the kind of joke he would have loved.' Gob: 'Oh, man!' [Sighs]
jokes.setup: Michael eulogizes Franklin with 'that's the kind of joke he would have loved'
jokes.punchline: Gob's genuine grief — the sigh and 'Oh, man!' — treats the loss of a hand puppet as a real bereavement
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional coda — Gob's genuine grief for Franklin is funny because he treats the puppet as a real person, but also oddly touching. Michael playing along ('that's the kind of joke he would have loved') makes it work as both comedy and brother-bonding.

jokes.text: 'Please don't do this.' [All right.] [Grunting] — Gob making Franklin do ventriloquist recovery exercises to restore the ether-damaged puppet's face
jokes.setup: Franklin's face has been damaged by ether
jokes.punchline: Gob is performing what appear to be mouth exercises on the puppet — the visual of a man carefully rehabilitating a hand puppet's face is absurd
jokes.explanation: A physical coda gag — Gob performing puppet rehabilitation. The image is inherently funny and the grunting adds to the commitment of the joke.

jokes.text: 'On the next season of Arrested Development' — the preview reveals Oscar going to prison after being framed by George Sr.
jokes.setup: The 'next episode' preview format
jokes.punchline: It's advertised as 'next season' — implying these events are season-finale consequential, treating Oscar going to prison as a major cliffhanger while characters casually accept it
jokes.explanation: The 'next season' framing is notable — it's a finale moment. The preview itself contains jokes (Oscar's burned fingerprints, Tobias's Vegas failure), playing the previews as comedy.

jokes.text: 'Oscar, robbed of his fingerprints, has difficulty proving his identity.' / Oscar: 'My hair's gonna grow back. You'll see.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. shaved Oscar's head to impersonate him
jokes.punchline: Oscar, now in prison with a shaved head and burned fingerprints (from the Cornballer), can't prove he's not George Sr. — the Cornballer injury has retroactively destroyed his alibi
jokes.explanation: Another extraordinary AD structural payoff. The Cornballer — established in previous episodes, used as a comedy prop, revisited for another burn — has burned off Oscar's fingerprints, preventing him from proving his identity. Combined with the shaved head, George Sr.'s con is airtight because of earlier comic details. Oscar's plea ('My hair's gonna grow back. You'll see.') is both pathetic and funny.

jokes.text: Tobias and Kitty go to Vegas only to find the Blue Man Group role has been filled: 'You found someone already?' / 'Just came out of nowhere, this guy. And the great thing is, he's never out of character.'
jokes.setup: Tobias sacrificed his marriage to pursue the Blue Man Group audition
jokes.punchline: The role is already filled by someone who is naturally blue (implied: someone actually committed to never breaking character) — Tobias, who is constantly breaking his own commitments, loses to someone more committed
jokes.explanation: The payoff to Tobias's season-long arc is that the role is filled by someone 'never out of character' — implying someone permanently blue (or more committed than Tobias). The irony of Tobias, who destroys relationships for acting dreams, being beaten by someone more dedicated is perfect.

jokes.text: George Sr. turns out to be right about the effects of stress: shown apparently losing his hair in prison — 'What's happening to me? Why won't it grow back?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. claimed Oscar only has hair because he's never had stress; George is now in a stress-free environment (prison, where he was happy) while Oscar is in stress
jokes.punchline: George is somehow losing his hair in what should be a comfortable situation — OR Oscar is losing his hair under prison stress, proving George's theory while ruining Oscar's distinguishing feature
jokes.explanation: Elegant callback to George's earlier observation about Oscar's hair. The theory being proven correct — in a way that destroys Oscar — is darkly satisfying. The question 'Why won't it grow back?' on a shaved head is also a callback to the head-shaving.

tmdb_overview: Gob hides George Sr. while the model home's plumbing is checked; George Michael helps Ann protest Maeby's new movie; Tobias takes a liking to Kitty after Michael asks him to keep an eye on her.
insight: Musical callback layering delivers 84 points through pure character escalation.
